hadn't done a workout in a long time.

I'm dealing with.

I spilled out that strategy, and I know you're literally an expert in AI, but also you're a serial CMO, and I thought that this team would love to hear from you as an ex-CMO, who's now a very successful in a very short amount of time CEO, running one of the hottest growth startups in AI, and we're working with you guys.

I'd like talk a little bit about you, about how you think about AI, your journey, how we should be in it as a marketing team, and would love you to just sort of, you know, take us behind the woodshed, man, and tell us.

Yeah, yeah.

So I'll go super fast way back, but I started a career early on at IBM, and I think one of the first things I realized when you're inside a large company like that, was like marketing was just so empty, and so I think the first insight I always had was,
Why does an IBM take that $1 billion a year that invests in marketing and ads and just buys every publication for all the audiences they care about?
That would actually be a better investment.
But they never do that, right?
And so I always set out to just try to get brands and marketing organizations to be more like publishers and leaders to be more like creators, you know?
And it was just like a way for me to also build little startups inside of big companies.
And so I had built this publication called SecureIntelligence.com, ended up doing really well.
And so like kind of fast forward, think that just kind of followed me every step of my journey.
And it was, I think, the thing that kind of set me apart always was like I always had these big bats, which were more like media company-like building big bats.

My marketing team was two people.
My first day, um, the.

And I was like, what's my budget?
Zero.
Okay.

No.

100 million, 300 million?
Like, yeah.
I was like, oh, actually, you do have budget.
And we have this virtual conference in 60 days.
And we already announced the date.
Good luck.
And I was like, what do I do?
So I think, like, I always kind of thought of, like, how do I play to our advantage, right?
Like, how do I do something that kind of makes this splash?
But not just like a splash just for kind of like a hack, but more of like, how do you build compounding value in marketing?
And I always think about there's a lot of things we do in marketing.
What are the things that we can do that the more we do, the better, cheaper, faster it gets, and the more it adds up versus what are the.
We do that might be necessary, but don't actually compound their diminishing returns that so that the more you do them, the less effective they get.
Right.
And so for me, I always just try to apply those human principles to marketing.
it's like, how do you build trust with people in your network?
Like, you know, you try to create value, give more than you take and things like that, and then applying that to to marketing.
So then fast forward, the story there in scale is like I did this conference in 60 days.
We got 10,000 people, Sam Altman and a bunch of people.

And, you know, and then my wife and I were going through just this really hard IVF.

Journey, and we have a two-year-old daughter, Isabella, but she was actually our last embryo, and my wife was in construction, which is a horrible industry for women, and under like a really toxic CEO as well, and so like both of us, I was at scale, she was in construction, and then IVF, so then she took medical leave, and then like at the end of that two years, you know, it's three years away from my path leave, and I left scale, and I just remember like having all this PTSD, and luckily I landed at Deepgram, which was a great company, and you know, but I think after I came back from my path leave, I was just like, I just want to reinvent myself a little bit, and then I just found myself like when I was in meetings, just being like, what are we doing here, like what is the point of this thing, you know, so I started to just like buy back my time, and so I hire an EA, and offshore, but she's amazing, and you know, and then I hire a second one, and then
I started to just cluster my days and delegate my days away and just got really smart.
So I essentially cleared out two days a week of my time.
And at first it was just spend a little bit more time with my daughter.
But then over time I was like, okay, now I'm like energized again.
I want to build something.
And that's when I just started building AI workflows and playing with AI.
And, you know, and I probably spent over a thousand hours.
And I think the part that was really cool was I was like all the thought process I had, like hiring editors and building teams.

And like I would show them, hey, okay, here's how we think about our audience.
Here's how we craft a strategy.
Here's how you find the opportunities.
Here's how you find a gap in those topics so that you can be a better answer to it.
So all these little thought processes that I had, I started to decompose them.
And it just felt like playing Legos again.
was obsessed with Legos as a kid, you know, and kind of testing and playing and changing the inputs like, whoa, this is really cool.
And then when I got it to a good point, I'm like, okay, now I can just hire the same people I used to hire, but now they have these guardrails of my thoughts, you know, and my processes.
And so we did that, ended up publishing 3,000 pages in 24-hour traffic.
And so then a lot of people started asking and started doing these workshops, these paid workshops.

have a baby at home, a mortgage.

And, you know, it's like from, from pretty much zero in a year.
And so, so it's been, it's been fun.

Oh, yeah, yeah.

I think, you you said some interesting things, which I, I always.

And maybe you could just sort of talk about like kind of how you got yourself like AI literate, you know, that what are the 10,000 hours and I wasn't that?

And not just like, I think there's two parts that there's, how did you get yourself started?
And then what, from a marketing perspective, are good places to focus, like where can you gain less, you know?
Yeah, the, I think the, the.
First lesson is you can't go deep in AI 30 minutes at a time.
You really can't, like, you know, especially if you're starting.

And so my biggest advice, I guess, or best advice is tell Marc you're taking two weeks off and say you're going on a vacation, but it's really a workation, you know, and go be gone for two weeks, gone, you know, and don't tell your partner or family that you're doing that either because otherwise they'll put you into the honeydew list.
But I'm joking, but that really helps.
It's just, like, not having the context switching.
That is just so hard because you're just not going to go deep and you just have to have that creative freedom to just not be worried about anything.
Checking Slack every five minutes, right?
Like, you just want to have that clarity of mind, wherever that looks like for you.
For me, it's like after first cup of coffee, first thing in the morning, and I haven't checked anything, and then, like, I get some level of inspiration or something.
I inspiration.
Like, some
Or it might be like driving to somewhere or, you know, whatever it might be, right?
Like that's the zone that is like your productive zone.
And then like the thing for me was breaking things down first principles that you feel like you're truly an expert in and that you know what great looks like, right?
Like if you've never tasted wine and you never tasted great wine and you never, you know, like you're not going to, you know, know what bad and great looks like, you know, just have to taste a lot.

But if you've done something a lot and you feel like a domain expert in that and you're passionate about and you're energized by that, then that's the perfect place to start, start to decompose, right?
And I think what happens is like not to pick on product marketing, but let's just use product marketing for an example.
Like I've had product marketers that in the past that would say, Hey, like, let's do more customer stories, for example.
Right.
And, and like, sometimes the way you think
Stuck.

Okay, hey, I need to think about like what customers to hit up.
Okay, cool.

Like, let's go down that path.
Like, how do I come up with the right questions to, to ask my customers?
And then let me try a bunch of different things.
Let me try a bunch of different inputs.
Let me try, you know, different thought processes.

And the more you can kind of decompose everything into first principles so that you can recompose it a little bit differently, then you can start taking the bits and pieces and start playing with different models and AI.
I can show you all some stuff too.
And then the last thing, or not the last thing, but the next thing I would say is the first principles.
The second is delegation, right?
You all are leaders.
So you all have been managers.
And delegated.
But actually, a lot of people are pretty bad at delegation.
And the way I think about it is, if the context you're giving AI is one where a human, an intern joining you, wouldn't be successful at that, then you're not doing a good job at delegating either.
So you can't delegate to AI something that you don't know how to delegate to another human successfully.
And so part of that is the big picture.
That's why context engineering is becoming more common, right?
Because these models are getting so good.
It's not about like the little, like, I'll pay you a million dollars if you do this right for me.
It's about like, what is the big picture?

And as leaders, like, we know the big picture better, but we need to be able to communicate the big picture in a way that distills it down to the essence and doesn't just, like, overwhelm people, right?
Like, so the way to think about it, a lot of AI, they're really kind of like this nice but overconfident intern.
That will have, you know, highly confident that they know the right answer.
And they will tell you they know the right answer, even if it's wrong, because they want to be right.
You know, so you got to treat AI like this overconfident intern with really high potential, you know, that absorbs everything you say.
And if you give it the right context and you put the right guardrails, right?
So then delegation is like, what is the craft of delegating successfully, right?
It's about context.
It's about like giving it the, what does done look like?
What does great look like?
How do you evaluate what great looks like?
Why do you think it that way, right?
What are the right inputs?
How do you criticize those inputs?
You know, what are the principles behind what's driving those decisions, right?
And so like that, those are all the right principles and decomposing it down to like the right level so that you're not just giving them like you hire an intern, right?
You're not going to give them, Hey, we really need to increase pipeline.
Like what are they going to do with that?
Right.
You're probably going to break it down a lot further.
So that's why those two connect.
And then the last one I would say that is like super important is like writing.
And it never felt like I was a great writer or like a writer at all.
But for me, because I've always been more of a verbal communicator, but almost like my whole career, most marketers on my team and people have interacted with like, oh, yeah, I'm not a great writer.
And it's like, we just need to like throw that away now and just say like writing is just an exercise and you need to build up the muscle.
And writing is the process to organize your thoughts.
And if you don't organize your thoughts, you can't do anything successfully.
And it's not because of AI I write less.
It's like because of AI, I'm able to organize my thoughts even better and find my own voice in the way I want to communicate things.
And if you, I think like for me, it's like, those are the three things that even as you're scaling, we're running literally millions of workflows.
We're building all these things.
We're doing all of that.
We're publishing 10 million plus words.
Like all these three things still apply to every part of what we're doing in every process, you know?

And so it's, and you notice I barely talked about models or tools, but really it's like what it comes down to in my opinion, you know, because the tools are shifting quickly and it kind of matters slightly in my opinion.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Super interesting.
I've got one more question and then I know you said you might want to shift some stuff and I also want to give the team a chance to ask you some questions.
But my one question to you would be, so think back to like you're helping us right now with essentially our inbound strategy, let's just say at the highest level.
It's really at the end of the day what this is, right?
So working on, you know, keyword selection and we're creating, you know, all of the different artifacts, the content assets that we need.
You guys are generating content for us, which we're then utilizing to optimize and improve search, get ourselves into GPT rankings, all that kind of stuff, right?
So you picked, you have picked a specific lane.
You go to swim in and go build a business around and you're doing it like ridiculously successfully.
And I will just tell the team and Marcel's working with some of the hottest companies in the world.
Like we're both in Silicon Valley and he's got a who's who list of clients.
It's super impressive for only being in business a year, right?
Something like that.
It's crazy.
But you picked a specific work club.
So here's the question.
Let's just say for some crazy reason, you decided to go back to being a CMO, which you would never do now.
But if you did, what are the areas where you would apply AI as a CMO outside of what you do today?
Meaning outside of what you do at GrowthX today?
So like it always goes back to first principles for me.
Every company, including our clients that we start, they don't have the foundation well defined.
And what I mean by that is like most companies work with, like we kicked off with like Sentinel One last week.
Outreach, Diligent.com, like all these companies, right?
Like you come in and you're like, hey, can you give me a good description that you feel really proud of your company?
It's like, you know, there's the website, but it's being updated.
It's being refreshed.
Oh, there's this.
It's like, it's all over the place, right?
Like, and if you ask like 20 people to give you an elevator pitch, you're going to get 20 bad answers, really, right?
And then you say like, who are like, like, tell me about your product and what are the jobs to be done that it solves for?
What are the pain points?
are, right?
Like you start to dig in on these like really foundational things and, and it's not well-defined and it's not written down often.
And if it is, it's inconsistent and all over the place.
And it's certainly not internalized by all the team members, right?
So for me, like the first thing, no matter what as a CMO, but it's also what we do for clients, but it's like what I recommend to everyone.
It's like have shared artifacts.
And if you have shared artifacts, then at least everyone using AI in your team.
will come from the first big picture, right?
Regardless of what models, what tools they're using, like, we all agree on this big picture.
Like, there's customers now that we're kicking off and, like, we're week four and we're in version, like, 11 of their artifacts, right?
And it's just, like, it requires that.
Sorry to interrupt, just for the, everyone may know what an artifact is, just explain what that is for them.
Yeah, yeah, absolutely.
Here, I can show you all as well, but, so, for instance, like, this is for you all, but, an artifact can be, like, a company context, for example, right?
And still, it's, like, company overview, elevator pitch, overview of features, you know.
It can be, like, your ideal customer, jobs to be done, type of thing, advantages.
It's really, like, what is the thing, like, defining, like, the context, the big picture of your company, right?
It can be, like, your personas, for instance, right?
But it's not just like, oh, give me a one-liner persona, but it's like, hey, their biggest fears and uncertainties, what motivates them deep inside?
How do you win their trust?
What will destroy their trust?
What do they care about the most related to their job?
What the biggest pain points and frustrations?
Those are the things that we found are commonalities.
But like anything you're doing, right?
Like if you're going towards a vertical, like, hey, do you have an artifact to define that vertical, right?
Like if you're, or how do you want to come across?
Like, you know, and so like these writing guidelines.
And then like what I do, like, and this is what we do internally as well for everything.
It's like, we'll create a, like a shared project and then just dump all of this artifact there, right?

And that alone is like a massive, massive difference, you know, because now, and you can set different ones with different instructions, but like now at least like, you know, it's coming from the same context, right?
And so for me, like that's the foundation is like, hey, do we have the same big picture?
And then from there, like the way I kind of think about it, it's like, it's a little bit counterintuitive to most people.
It's like, how do you avoid buying any tools whatsoever and avoid the like, let's go do big purchases of tools and things like that.
Like I can, I will give you the stack that I use every day and like, I don't need anything else.

And, and it's, so it's not about the tools at all.
Actually, like you can go into Clot and use their Clot playground, you know, and it's like, it's just like, you actually want to use less over time of these big systems that do a bunch of things.
They're so obfuscated, right?
And, and like a second, a new model comes out, whatever you're using, like, if it's not directly that model, you're already behind.
Because now they're going to take three or months to update that and use that, right?
so, so you kind of want to go direct to the source is my, my opinion.
And so for me.
Like, that's the first thing.

there's, like, perplexity is a great tool, for instance.

Right.

Right.
So like really like where I would apply a lot of AI is like going and fetching a lot of information, doing deep research, using some agents and then trying to accelerate the processes that you have.
So for instance, for us, it's like transcripts, right?
Like anytime I'm having a conversation, I'm recording and transcribing.
So then it's like how do you take that transcript and store it in a place that can be accessed by everyone and used, but also with that context.
So then like if you take a transcript of a call and you just throw it into a cloud project and you say, hey, help me understand what you heard and give me a summary.
It's already going to have all this context.

Right.
And then like what we do from there is like try to create like, uh,
versions of the ad that are functional.

And now anytime we're shaping a product, you know, we're having a conversation, we'll transcribe and throw it into that project.
And it just accelerates the time to like a really good output, right?
And so like, those are really like kind of the areas.
It's like decomposing, having shared artifacts and context, and then using these tools for the things that they're really, really good at, which is like research.
And there's some agentic ones that really like, this is great, you know, and then like whisper flow for transcription within your desktop that you just click a key and talk.

just a recent one, maybe.

they came to us saying like, hey, we did all the study, all this work.
It's beautiful.

Like we need to reflect what we did.
But that was like a pretty hard challenge because.

And so one of the first things we did, we just started doing these deep dives with them and just asking a lot of questions.
And then we did this two-hour session with their chief medical officer or the person that developed everything.
She's like this holistic doctor.
She's amazing, Maggie.
And just like we had taken all those artifacts, generated all these questions.
So we came in with like these really specific curated questions and she was so impressed.
And so then we went through and it was supposed to be an hour.
ended up spending two hours, but we got so deep.
And then from there, we started to iterate on the artifacts.
And so came up with the whole content strategy.
And the first article we generated for them, which this is like really technical and like very nuanced, like from a medical perspective, because you can't get it wrong.
But it's also like you want to get the wording right and they're deeply, they deeply care about their brand, you know, and this is like their first impression of their new brand.
And like really not.
Get out of the park.
But then she started to give like these really nuanced things.
Like for instance, like, hey, bad sleep doesn't just raise your levels of this.
It's really like if you consistently get bad sleep, then that's bad because of blah, blah, blah, blah.
It's like little nuanced things like that, right?
So we actually built a medical expert in the loop workflow based on the comments she gave and kind of decompose that.
So what we did was say like, hey, here's the set of all the comments she gave us on this article, which wasn't about voice and tone, like medical things.
And then we said, hey, here's this person's background.
But also here's the audience we care about.
Like what kind of experts, medical experts that would like be the right kind of people to review this.
And we just kind of like a mixture of experts where it's just like, hey, these are the experts.
And I want you all to take all these principles and like look at this and review and criticize based on essentially like this size.
And by the way, here's the comments this expert gave us before.
Try to think through like what's behind these.
And how could you do a better job editing this content in the future?
And so then, like, after we did that, like, the next pass, it was, like, so much simpler, right?
It wasn't 100%, but it was, like, whoa, this is really, really good.

Now, how do we decompose it so that the next time this person reviews this is going to be simpler because this person's time is very, very limited, right?
And, like, that's an example, you know, and so, like, that's a lot of kind of how we approach things when something is complex or hard is not to try to go from zero to 100%.
It's, like, okay, what's the next step that we can solve that then will get this even closer and closer, you know, to being more efficient?
Got it, cool.
Questions from ourselves?
What's, I don't know, I'll repeat, one second.

Yeah, like, I, like, we're pretty big on a lot of Claude models, you know, Sunnet and Opus, and, and I think it's like, what I do more is, like, I'll use perplexity for research and deep research, and then Manus for, like, really in-depth things.

And I just find myself using chat to BT less and less, but it's also more of a principle thing of just like, don't trust open AI too much.
I've interacted with Sam Altman.

I, yeah, yeah.
I've interacted with him a lot and behind the scenes and, and like, I, I, uh, from a principal's perspective, I appreciate.

Tropic a lot more, and so like, but yeah, I think it's always good to be testing different things, and so I kind of use opportunities when I'm writing something myself, like I'll sometimes we'll have like Grock, Chagipiti, and Claude or something else, and like I'll try the same prompts in parallel, and just like, that's the experimentation, like anytime you're writing something, doing something, just use it as a way to kind of experiment, you know, and try new things.

I think of it as like, every work you're doing is just R&D too, to try to get a better, a better sense, you know, and then I use V0 quite a bit these days, and that's, there's some fun ones, if we have time, I can show, I can share you one that I did, that's like really cool, two minutes, I can, I can, oh, yeah, no, we're gonna have to wrap here, I'd say.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[AI discussion with Marcel Santilli, CEO of GrowthX, to provide insights on AI applications in marketing for Rapyd's marketing leadership team.](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [AI literacy requires dedicated, uninterrupted time to explore and decompose processes into first principles](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=10.0)
  - [Effective AI use in marketing starts with creating shared "artifacts" that define company context, personas, and guidelines](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=729.0)
  - [Focus on using AI for research, information synthesis, and accelerating existing processes rather than replacing them entirely](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1382.0)
  - [AI tools should be viewed as assistants to augment human work, not fully autonomous solutions](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1541.0)

## Topics

### Marcel's Background and Journey to AI

  - [Started career at IBM, always focused on making marketing more like publishing](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=163.0)
  - [Successful CMO experiences at Scale AI and Deepgram](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=228.0)
  - [Transitioned to CEO of GrowthX after exploring AI workflows during personal time](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=338.0)
  - [GrowthX grew to 70 people and $15M ARR in first year without marketing spend](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=575.0)

### Becoming AI Literate

  - [Dedicate uninterrupted time (e.g., 2 weeks) to deeply explore AI without distractions](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=665.0)
  - [Focus on decomposing processes you're expert in into first principles](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=729.0)
  - [Practice effective delegation by providing clear context and expectations](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=922.0)
  - [Improve writing skills to better organize thoughts and communicate with AI](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=976.0)

### AI Applications in Marketing

  - [Create shared "artifacts" defining company context, personas, features, etc.](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1192.0)
  - [Use AI for deep research and information synthesis (e.g., Perplexity, Claude)](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1410.0)
  - [Accelerate existing processes like transcript analysis and content creation](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1468.0)
  - [Implement "expert-in-the-loop" workflows to refine AI outputs](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1692.0)

### AI Tools and Models

  - [Favors Claude (Anthropic) models for most tasks](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1947.0)
  - [Uses Perplexity for research, Manus for complex multi-step tasks](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1947.0)
  - [Recommends experimenting with multiple models in parallel](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=2008.0)
  - [Cautions against over-reliance on OpenAI/ChatGPT](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1969.0)

### Marketing Investment Priorities

  - [Focus on creating lasting, compounding value (e.g., high-quality content, podcasts)](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1184.0)
  - [Invest in polished design and presentation to stand out](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=2220.0)
  - [Create educational workshops and events to build trust and relationships](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=2253.0)
  - [Develop programs that foster community and ecosystem growth](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=2277.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Team members to connect with Marcel on LinkedIn for follow-up questions](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=2310.0)
  - [Consider implementing shared "artifacts" for Rapyd's marketing efforts](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1192.0)
  - [Explore dedicating focused time for team members to become more AI literate](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=665.0)
  - [Evaluate current marketing processes for potential AI acceleration opportunities](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=1382.0)
  - [Investigate educational content/event opportunities to build trust with Rapyd's audience](https://fathom.video/share/X5Axrt8sWhx36oWUqV3Ck_SceahbnHUb?tab=summary&timestamp=2253.0)
